fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content:
Professor Andrew Seidl plays a global leadership role as Senior Technical Advisor to the Biodiversity Finance Initiative. Through this work, he supports countries around the world in designing and implementing innovative finance solutions to close critical funding gaps for biodiversity conservation. 
The Biodiversity Finance Initiative (BIOFIN), led by the United Nations Development Programme, helps governments assess their biodiversity finance needs, reform policies, and mobilize new public and private investment to protect ecosystems and achieve global conservation targets.  Seidl contributes technical expertise in economic valuation, policy design, and finance strategies, helping translate complex environmental challenges into actionable, country-level investment plans. 
His work spans global research, capacity building, and on-the-ground advising, advancing a shift in how nations value nature and integrate biodiversity into economic decision-making. Through BIOFIN, these efforts have contributed to billions of dollars in financing for nature and support a growing international movement to align financial systems with sustainability and conservation goals.

Brief Summary.value: Professor Seidl advises BIOFIN, helping countries design finance solutions for biodiversity, reform policies, and mobilize investment, advancing global efforts to integrate conservation into economic decision-making and policy.

fields.Card Image Alt Text: A person works in a green field with a variety of plants with trees in the background

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content:
The Colorado GROW Youth Institute is a statewide program that empowers high school students to explore and address pressing food security challenges locally and globally. Launched in 2024 in partnership with the World Food Prize Foundation, the program engages students in researching a global challenge, producing a paper, and presenting their ideas in roundtable discussions with peers and professionals at the annual event hosted at the CSU Spur campus.
Participants also engage in interactive, hands-on learning and explore academic and career pathways at the intersection of agriculture, sustainability, and food security. Outstanding student presenters are selected to represent Colorado as delegates at the World Food Prize Foundation’s Global Youth Institute. All participants are considered for scholarships to CSU's College of Agricultural Sciences and may apply for additional opportunities provided by the World Food Prize Foundation.
Beyond the annual event, the program fosters continued leadership development for alumni and provides educators with resources and support to integrate the Global Challenge curriculum into their classrooms.

Brief Summary.value: The Colorado GROW Youth Institute empowers high school students to address food security challenges through research, presentations, hands-on learning, and leadership development, with opportunities for scholarships and global engagement.

fields.Card Image Alt Text: Students participants in the Colorado GROW Youth Institute pose for a group photo.

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content: Dr. Steven Fonte works with farming communities in three highland regions (Junín, Huancavelica, and Ayacucho) of the Peruvian Andes to develop sustainable pathways for agroecological intensification.  This work includes the co-development of forage-based fallows, in collaboration with farmers, as well as evaluation of other promising management options (e.g., agroforestry, diversification) to support soil health, long-term productivity and farmer livelihoods.

Brief Summary.value: Dr. Fonte collaborates with Peruvian Andean farmers to develop sustainable agroecological practices, including forage-based fallows and agroforestry, aiming to improve soil health, productivity, and farmer livelihoods.

fields.Card Image Alt Text: A view of mountainous farmland in Peru

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content: Through this project, Dr. Steven Fonte focuses on supporting improved research and awareness of soil related challenges in smallholder farming systems of Latin America and Africa. The project is working to refine a soil health tool kit and related products for use in smallholder farming contexts. This work involves continued validation and refinement of tool kit methods, participatory research with farmer groups and collaborating organizations.

Brief Summary.value: Dr. Steven Fonte’s project refines a soil health toolkit for smallholder farmers in Latin America and Africa, emphasizing participatory research, validation, and increased awareness of soil challenges.

fields.Card Image Alt Text: pink liquid in test tubes

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content: Andy Seidl is a member of the Economics of Land Degradation (ELD) Working Group. Under the mission statement “_Linking science to policy and practice for sustainable land management_” the ELD Working Group supports the United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification in part through the publication of the Global Land Outlook, economic analyses of pastoral landscapes, contributions to the Ecosystem Services Valuation Database (ESVD) and the planned development of a spatially explicit land restoration investment platform.
fields.Other Partners or Collaborators: united nations convention to combat desertification (UNCCD)

Brief Summary.value: Andy Seidl contributes to the ELD Working Group, supporting sustainable land management through economic analyses, publications, and developing tools for land restoration investment aligned with UN desertification efforts.

fields.Card Image Alt Text: Cracked soil with a dead tree and ominous clouds

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content: Andy Seidl serves on the Sustainable Finance Specialist Group and the UNESCO Natural World Heritage Site Specialist Group of the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) World Commission on Protected Areas (WCPA). These groups provide review, research, training, and advice on the establishment and management of parks and protected areas worldwide. They actively participate in the planning and execution of the World Conservation Congress and the World Parks Congress.
fields.Other Partners or Collaborators: International Union for Conservation of Nature

Brief Summary.value: Andy Seidl contributes expertise to IUCN WCPA specialist groups, supporting global protected area management through research, training, and participation in major conservation congresses. Target audience: college leadership.

fields.Card Image Alt Text: A natural landscape with a small river, tall pine trees, and mountains in the background

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content:
Understanding the interplay between biodiversity and climate change is critical for the implementation of effective and lasting solutions to climate change and for maintaining biodiversity and nature’s contributions to people. The USGS was charged by the US Congress in the Fiscal Year 2022 budget with conducting an assessment that characterizes the state of understanding concerning linkages between biodiversity and climate change for the United States. Drivers of biodiversity loss and climate change impacts cross national boundaries, and accordingly the geographic scope of assessment will include neighboring nations, particularly Canada and Mexico. 
Andy Seidl provided direct inputs on transboundary finance mechanisms and policies to the report. USGS is the primary convenor of this assessment with strong involvement from CONAMP and other agencies of the Mexican and Canadian governments and scholars from first nations/Indigenous people.

Brief Summary.value: USGS leads a congressionally mandated assessment on biodiversity-climate change linkages in the US, Canada, and Mexico, with input on transboundary finance and Indigenous perspectives for effective solutions.

fields.Project/Activity Title: North American Biodiversity and Climate Change Assessment
fields.Card Image Alt Text: A desert plant in cracked red soil

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content:
For more than 25 years, Colorado State University and Lincoln University have sustained a dynamic exchange partnership that offers students an immersive, hands-on international learning experience. Participants study in a close-knit academic environment known for its strengths in agriculture, environmental systems, and sustainability, while engaging in field-based coursework and exploring the diverse landscapes of New Zealand.
Each spring semester, a CSU Faculty-in-Residence accompanies students, providing on-site academic support and fostering a strong sense of community abroad. The program also allows students to earn transferable credits while gaining a global perspective and building international networks.
In 2025, the partnership expanded to include a U.S.-based ranch internship funded by Angus New Zealand, offering participants hands-on industry experience and a unique opportunity to connect international study with applied agricultural practice.

Brief Summary.value: CSU and Lincoln University offer an immersive exchange focused on agriculture and sustainability, with fieldwork in New Zealand and a new U.S. ranch internship for hands-on industry experience.

fields.Card Image Alt Text: Lincoln University: A red brick building with white decorative edges

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content: This short-term CSU faculty-led program in Germany & Austria provides opportunity for students to follow the exploration of European culture and history through immersion in the European equine industry. An international focus on breeding, showing, managing and marketing of the horse is also an important part of this program. Students have opportunities to visit several horse breeding and training facilities, major manufacturers of equipment, as well as observe training sessions, plus a variety of local cultural activities.

Brief Summary.value: This CSU faculty-led program in Germany and Austria immerses students in European equine industry practices, including breeding, management, marketing, facility visits, and cultural activities.

fields.Card Image Alt Text: A brown horse with a rider walks down a hill in a competition arena with an audience in the stands

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content:
This CSU faculty-led fall semester program takes students to live and learn in Florence, Italy. Students study Food & Resource Economics through experiential learning opportunities that go beyond the traditional classroom setting.  
Florence is located in the Tuscany region of Italy, on the banks of the Arno River, surrounded by the rolling hills of the Italian countryside. The city has the essence of the Renaissance and stands out for its culture and history. Florence museums, palaces and churches house some of the greatest artistic treasures in the world.  
Coursework integrates economics, policy, and culture, exploring how Italian food and resource-based industries are shaped by history, markets, and sustainability practices. Students engage in experiential learning through field-based activities and required excursions that connect classroom concepts to real-world contexts across Florence and the surrounding countryside. 
Beyond academics, participants experience daily life in Italy—gaining firsthand insight into local food systems, regional culture, and the role of place in economic development—while building a global perspective on agriculture, sustainability, and international markets.

Brief Summary.value: CSU’s fall program in Florence immerses students in Food & Resource Economics through experiential learning, integrating coursework, field activities, and cultural experiences to explore Italian agriculture, sustainability, and markets.

fields.Project/Activity Title: Food and Resource Economics in Italy
fields.Card Image Alt Text: An image of Florence, Italy prominently featuring the Florence Cathedral and other historic buildings with mountains in the distance

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content: This CSU faculty-led summer program gives participants direct exposure to the rich history of landscape design in Italy and France. Students will visit many important landscape architectural precedents and pursue research on various topics including land planning, urban design, community design, and insightful garden design for public and private places. Students will travel throughout Italy and France to explore architecture in many different cities including Venice, Tuscany, Florence, Rome, Versailles, Normandy and Paris. Specific site visits may include classical landscapes in and around Rome, medieval hill towns of Tuscany, remarkable private gardens and villas of the renaissance near Florence, royal parks of Louis XIV in central France, and the modern public gardens of Paris.

Brief Summary.value: CSU faculty-led summer program offers students hands-on study of historic landscape design in Italy and France, including site visits, research, and exploration of urban and garden design.

fields.Project/Activity Title: Landscape Architecture in Italy and France
fields.Card Image Alt Text: Italian village Civita di Bagnoregio

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content:
The University of Glasgow's School of Veterinary Medicine, has an internationally renowned reputation in Veterinary Medicine, and is fully accredited by the AVMA. CSU is working with The University of Glasgow to offer a unique opportunity for high quality animal science and equine science students who wish to study veterinary medicine within the global context.
Selected students will study the final year of their animal science/equine science degree at the University of Glasgow as a study abroad opportunity. During their time at Glasgow, CSU students will study alongside students in their first year of the University of Glasgow’s veterinary medicine degree programme (Bachelor of Veterinary Medicine and Surgery, BVMS – which is the equivalent to the DVM).

Brief Summary.value: CSU partners with University of Glasgow to offer animal and equine science students a study abroad year, integrating with Glasgow’s veterinary medicine program for global veterinary education experience.

fields.Card Image Alt Text: Glasgow University Building

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content:
A sabbatical at Chalmers University in Gothenburg, Sweden--in the Chalmers School of Entrepreneurship--inspired both a new graduate program and a new line of research at CSU. 
The masters in Agribusiness and Food Innovation Management was developed by Dr. Graff and colleagues in the department of Agricultural and Resource Economics to be offered at CSU Spur. The program adapted key design elements from the Chalmers masters in entrepreneurship, including a team-based degree project in which students develop and seek to launch a new product, technology, or even a startup. Instructors in Sweden have regularly provided guest lectures in this CSU program.
Dr. Graff has also collaborated with faculty and Ph.D. students at Chalmers on research to understand how the dynamics of the venture financing lifecycle influences the success and thus the economic impact of startups. This line of research led to a recently published study on the venture financing of agtech startups in the U.S.

Brief Summary.value: Dr. Graff’s sabbatical at Chalmers inspired CSU’s new Agribusiness master’s program and research on venture financing, fostering international collaboration and innovative, team-based student projects.

fields.Card Image Alt Text: Chalmers University in Sweden

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content:
The key differences between introduced species that become harmful invaders and those who do not is an ongoing research question. In this project, Hufbauer and colleagues from France, New Zealand, and Australia study whether species that adapt to human-altered environments in their native regions are more likely to thrive in similar environments when introduced elsewhere.
By combining field sampling, genetic analysis, and environmental data, the team examines how human activities—such as land use change—shape the traits that help species spread. Their findings show how these human-driven conditions can create predictable patterns that favor certain species becoming invasive.
This research helps scientists better understand and anticipate biological invasions, improving our ability to prevent and manage invasive species that can harm ecosystems, agriculture, and economies.

Brief Summary.value: Researchers study if species adapted to human-altered environments in their native regions are more likely to become invasive elsewhere, using fieldwork and genetics to predict and manage biological invasions.

fields.Card Image Alt Text: Close up of grass with weeds competing

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content: The Borowiec lab studies ants from around the world in collaboration with researchers from multiple countries to understand how ants evolved and became so diverse and successful. The lab uses large-scale DNA data to build detailed evolutionary trees, showing both how different groups are related and how similar traits evolve independently in different groups. This work helps explain broad patterns in biodiversity and how species adapt to their environments, which is important for understanding ecosystems globally.

Brief Summary.value: The Borowiec lab uses global DNA data to map ant evolution, revealing relationships, trait development, and biodiversity patterns, aiding understanding of species adaptation and ecosystem function worldwide.

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content: The CSU Potato Improvement Program shares potato germplasm and exchanges unselected seedling potato families with the Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada National Potato Breeding Program at the Fredericton Research and Development Centre. This collaboration allows the program to broaden the genetic diversity of the parental material, ensuring the development of resilient potato varieties. The exchange of unselected material between the Canada Potato Breeding Program and CSU is an efficient method to produce a high volume of seedling tubers, as well as increasing the likelihood of identifying elite cultivars adapted to either region, benefiting both programs.

Brief Summary.value: CSU collaborates with Canada’s Potato Breeding Program to exchange germplasm, broadening genetic diversity and efficiently developing resilient, regionally adapted potato varieties for mutual benefit.

fields.Card Image Alt Text: Bags of several varieties and colors of potatos

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content:
The Ant Course is a globally recognized training program that brings together students, researchers, and professionals to learn directly from leading myrmecologists through a combination of lectures, laboratory work, and hands-on field experience. Participants gain practical skills in ant identification, sampling, specimen curation, and the study of ant diversity, behavior, and their roles in ecosystems. The October 2026 Ant Course will take place at CSU's Todos Santos campus in Mexico.
The course emphasizes ant phylogeny, diversity, and classification; social behavior and colony biology; field collection and biodiversity sampling methods; and specimen preparation and identification techniques.
The program is designed for a broad audience, including students, postdocs, professors, researchers, and motivated participants from diverse backgrounds in biology, including those with no previous experience with ants.

Brief Summary.value: The Ant Course at CSU Todos Santos offers hands-on training in ant identification, diversity, behavior, and field methods for students, researchers, and professionals from diverse biology backgrounds.

fields.Card Image Alt Text: The words "Ant Course" in black with the silhouette of an ant through the words on a backgroud of a close up ant image

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content: University Distinguished Professor Jan Leach serves as a scientific advisor for the International Genetic Engineering and Biotechnology Center (ICGEB), an intergovernmental organization advancing research, training, and technology transfer in Life Sciences and Biotechnology. In this role, Dr. Leach steers and evaluates the research endeavors of the center to help align their research efforts with the goals of the organization. The center has laboratories located in Cape Town, South Africa; Trieste, Italy; and Delhi, India where they conduct research on a variety of topics including plant biology and biotechnology. The ICGEB connects nearly 70 countries with these research and training opportunities to address global sustainable development issues including hunger, health, energy, and education.

Brief Summary.value: Professor Jan Leach advises ICGEB, guiding research and training in life sciences and biotechnology across global labs to address sustainable development challenges in health, hunger, energy, and education.

fields.Project/Activity Title: International Center for Genetic Engineering and Biotechnology
fields.Card Image Alt Text: A group photo of the scientific advisors at the International Center for Genetic Engineering and Biotechnology

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content:
Through a USDA NIFA-funded partnership with the International Rice Research Institute (IRRI) in the Philippines, University Distinguished Professor Jan Leach's lab is exploring an overlooked question: how do disease-resistant rice plants affect not just harmful pathogens, but also the beneficial microbes that live in, on and around the plant. The issue is similar to what happens in humans when we take antibiotics. While antibiotics kill harmful bacteria, they can also disrupt the body’s healthy microbial community. 
The Leach lab is asking whether breeding crops for disease resistance has a similar unintended effect. When plants are bred to resist disease, they activate defense responses that produce chemicals that help fight off pathogens—but those same chemicals may also reshape the plant’s microbiome. Until now, little attention has been paid to these broader impacts. To address this gap, the team is studying rice plants with different resistance genes and tracking how their microbial communities change over time. They are also examining how these changes affect plant health and performance in the long term. 
This work has implications beyond plant disease. Rice paddies are one of the world's largest agricultural sources of methane, a potent greenhouse gas. Methane is produced not by the rice plants themselves, but by specialized microbes living in the flooded soil and around rice roots. The composition of these microbial communities can influence how much methane is generated and released into the atmosphere. By understanding how disease-resistance traits alter plant-associated microbial communities, researchers may uncover new strategies to reduce methane emissions while maintaining strong disease resistance. 
In collaboration with partners in Taiwan, the CSU-IRRI team is measuring how resistance-related changes in the rice microbiome affect methane production and emissions. Together, this research is opening a new way of thinking about crop improvement—one that considers not only the genetics of the plant, but also the microbial communities that support it, with important implications for both global food security and climate resilience.

Brief Summary.value: Leach lab studies how breeding rice for disease resistance impacts beneficial microbes and methane emissions, aiming to improve crop health and reduce greenhouse gases for food security and climate resilience.

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content: University Distinguished Professor Jan Leach serves as a Board Member for the International Phytobiomes Alliance, an international, nonprofit alliance of industry, academic, and governmental partners that facilitates and coordinates international efforts toward expanding phytobiomes research. A phytobiome refers to the complex ecosystem formed by plants and their surrounding environment, including microbes, insects, animals, and other plants. By promoting phytobiome health, the Alliance supports more resilient and sustainable agricultural systems. Its work emphasizes how management decisions—from irrigation and pest control to crop breeding—shape these systems, and it provides research and education to help producers apply phytobiome-based practices in diverse global contexts.

Brief Summary.value: University Distinguished Professor Jan Leach serves on the International Phytobiomes Alliance Board, supporting global research and education to promote resilient, sustainable agriculture through phytobiome-based management and practices.

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content:
Plant breeders have traditionally focused on single disease resistance genes—acting as “generals”—that detect pathogens and signal downstream genes to mount a defense response. However, pathogens evolve over time to overcome these resistance genes, limiting the durability of this strategy. To address this challenge, University Distinguished Professor Jan Leach's lab is advancing a novel approach that shifts focus from the “generals” to the downstream “soldier” genes that execute defense responses. 
Rather than relying on pathogen recognition alone, this strategy enhances how effectively defense genes respond once triggered. By selecting for stronger gene promoters, the team aims to amplify and broaden activation of defense pathways, resulting in more robust and durable resistance. As Leach notes, “It actually works—we’ve shown we can select for better promoters of those genes, and it makes the plants more resistant.” 
Support from the Foundation for Food and Agricultural Research has enabled an ongoing international collaboration with the International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT) in Colombia. This partnership includes Colorado State University, the Universidad de Los Andes (Colombia), and the French National Research Institute for Sustainable Development (IRD). The project has delivered both scientific and capacity-building outcomes. It has generated genome sequences of key rice varieties and demonstrated the ability to identify and target genomic regions associated with enhanced defense responses. It has also trained graduate students and research associates across partner institutions and CGIAR centers. A three-day workshop at CIAT introduced plant breeders from across Latin America to this new strategy. Importantly, the project has fostered new and ongoing interdisciplinary and international collaborations.

Brief Summary.value: Leach’s lab developed a novel approach to plant disease resistance by enhancing defense gene promoters, resulting in more durable resistance and international collaborations, training, and scientific advances in rice breeding.

fields.Card Image Alt Text: View of a green rice field in the Phillipines

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content: A growing share of the world’s rice is produced in Africa, where a destructive pathogen studied by the University Distinguished Professor Jan Leach's lab is causing significant and expanding damage. To address this emerging threat, the lab is working closely with the French National Research Institute for Sustainable Development (IRD). In Tanzania and neighboring countries, IRD researchers have used diagnostic tools (primers) developed by the Leach lab to confirm the presence of the pathogen, which continues to spread across the region. This collaboration has been critical in tracking the disease and understanding its impact on rice production. Dr. Leach contributes to global efforts to combat the pathogen by serving on a scientific advisory committee for a Gates Foundation–supported project focused on developing new forms of resistance. These efforts are now reaching a key milestone: newly developed resistant rice varieties are expected to be released soon, offering farmers a much-needed tool to protect their crops and livelihoods.

Brief Summary.value: Leach lab collaborates internationally to track and combat a destructive rice pathogen in Africa, leading to the imminent release of resistant rice varieties to protect regional agriculture.

fields.Card Image Alt Text: Three people working in a genome editing lab

fields.Project/Activity Summary - Story Content:
Quinoa is a traditional Andean crop with increasing global importance because of its nutritional value, drought tolerance, and potential role in climate-resilient agriculture. As quinoa production expands beyond its center of origin, however, growers are encountering new pest challenges that require locally adapted integrated pest management strategies. This project connects emerging quinoa pest issues in North America with the longer history of quinoa cultivation and pest management in Peru and the broader Andean region.
In collaboration with Prof. Luis Miguel Cruces at the Universidad Nacional Agraria La Molina in Lima, Peru, Dr. Szczepaniec's team is comparing quinoa pest management across Andean and North American production systems. This work has led to a collaborative manuscript, _Quinoa pest management across continents: Lessons from Andean systems and emerging threats in North America_, which examines how pest complexes differ between long-established Andean systems and newer quinoa production regions in the United States. The project highlights shared priorities, including improved monitoring, biological control, host plant resistance, and regionally appropriate IPM tools. 
This collaboration is also creating a foundation for future comparative research on whether quinoa varieties grown in Peru may express stronger resistance to emerging pests affecting quinoa production in the United States, including the stem-boring fly _Amauromyza karli_. More broadly, the project supports international scholarly exchange and helps build pest management strategies informed by both Andean experience and emerging North American production needs.

Brief Summary.value: This project compares quinoa pest management in Andean and North American systems, identifying shared priorities and fostering international collaboration to develop regionally adapted, climate-resilient integrated pest management strategies.
